title: Kähler Compactification of ℂn and Reeb Dynamics
speaker: Zhengyi Zhou
affiliation: Chinese Academy of Mathematics and Systems Science
field_msc: 32 Several complex variables and analytic spaces
abstract: We will present two results in complex geometry: (1) A Kähler compactification of ℂn with a smooth divisor complement must be ℙn, which confirms a conjecture of Brenton and Morrow under the Kähler assumption; (2) Any complete asymptotically conical Calabi-Yau metric on ℂ3 with a smooth link must be flat, confirming a modified version of Tian’s conjecture regarding the recognition of the flat metric among Calabi-Yau metrics in dimension 3. Both proofs rely on relating the minimal discrepancy number of a Fano cone singularity to its Reeb dynamics of the conic contact form. This is a joint work with Chi Li.

proof_stages.label: Smooth and orbifold compactification targets
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines compactifications (X,D) of C^n, explains why one restricts the classification problem, reviews known low-dimensional and projective results, states the smooth Kähler/projective standardness theorem, and formulates the orbifold analogue.

proof_stages.label: AC Kähler ends as Sasaki/Reeb quotients
proof_stages.summary: Sasaki and contact geometry are introduced so that an asymptotically conical Kähler end has a contact link whose Reeb flow, when quotiented or perturbed to a rational direction, naturally produces orbifold compactifications.

proof_stages.label: Minimum discrepancy and Fano cone singularities
proof_stages.summary: The algebraic input is set up by recalling q-Gorenstein isolated singularities, minimal discrepancy, McLean's contact-topological interpretation via first Chern classes, Fano cone singularities, Fano index, and the Kobayashi-Ochiai characterization needed later.

proof_stages.label: Technical theorem linking discrepancy to Reeb and symplectic homology data
proof_stages.summary: The main technical theorem states that for isolated Fano cone singularities the minimal discrepancy is the infimum of lower SFT degrees of closed Reeb orbits and that this extremal degree is detected in positive S^1-equivariant symplectic homology, with rational Conley-Zehnder indices and lower SFT degrees defined to make the statement precise.

proof_stages.label: Local quotient and Maslov-index computation of the equality
proof_stages.summary: The first equality is proved by computing minimal discrepancies from the local quotient models of the orbifold line bundle and matching them to lower SFT degrees through the Fano/Maslov index of principal Reeb orbits plus the necessary framing correction.

proof_stages.label: Symplectic-homology detection by a Morse-Bott spectral sequence
proof_stages.summary: The second part of the technical theorem is proved using exact sequences for symplectic homology and positive S^1-equivariant symplectic homology, together with a Morse-Bott spectral sequence in which parity and filtration force the minimal-degree generator to survive.

proof_stages.label: Application to smooth Kähler compactifications
proof_stages.summary: For a smooth divisor compactification, a strongly pseudoconvex contact boundary and an AC-induced Liouville cobordism to the standard sphere give vanishing symplectic homology and the same positive equivariant calculation as C^n, forcing minimal discrepancy n-1, Fano index n, and hence D biholomorphic to P^{n-1}.

proof_stages.label: AC Calabi-Yau flatness under Shokurov
proof_stages.summary: After Tian's original maximum-volume-growth conjecture is corrected by imposing a smooth AC link, Shokurov's conjecture for Fano cones and the Reeb/symplectic-homology theorem reduce the tangent cone to a smooth weighted linear Calabi-Yau cone; the Sasaki-Einstein volume condition forces equal weights and Anderson rigidity then makes the original metric flat.

examples.name: Tangent-hyperplane quadric compactifications
examples.note: Quadrics with tangent hyperplane divisors illustrate that complements biholomorphic to affine space need not come only from the standard projective compactification, motivating the classification problem and its restrictions.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker presents tangent-hyperplane quadrics as compactification examples and states their complements/divisors, but does not actually verify or compute these facts in the transcript

examples.name: Contact ellipsoids with rational or irrational Reeb flow
examples.note: The ellipsoid makes concrete how rational weights give closed Reeb orbits and a one-dimensional torus action, while irrational weights lead to higher-dimensional torus closures and a Morse-Bott picture.
examples.engagement_reason: contact ellipsoids are used as a basic illustration of rational versus irrational Reeb behavior, with the orbit-closure facts stated rather than derived or computed

passes.reason: Rational and irrational contact ellipsoids form an ordinary toric family used only to illustrate regular versus irregular Reeb dynamics.

examples.name: Yang Li's Calabi-Yau metric on C^3
examples.note: This counterexample with tangent cone C^2/Z_2 times C is used to show why Tian's original flatness conjecture must be strengthened by requiring a smooth Sasaki link at infinity.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the asymptotic-cone/link viewpoint to Yang Li’s metric, identifying the tangent cone C^2/Z_2 × C and reasoning that its Sasaki link has an S^1-family of singularities
